library_policy: Cataloging is not adoption. Items are not integrated without explicit project selection and validation.

items.name: Google Cloud Open Knowledge Format (OKF)
items.short_description: Open, vendor-neutral format for an agent- and human-readable project knowledge bundle: Markdown concept files with YAML front matter and links between tables, datasets, metrics, APIs, playbooks, and join paths.
items.why_it_matters: An optional way to give agents stable, reviewable context about a project's data model without making a database, cloud provider, vector store, or SDK part of the agent runtime.
agent_building_use_cases: Document project tables, views, datasets, metrics, ownership, definitions, and join paths as versioned files.
Create a compact context layer for database-aware agents before they read raw schemas or query systems.
Keep database meaning, data quality notes, access constraints, and runbooks reviewable alongside code.
items.license: Open specification; check the selected implementation and repository license before adopting tooling.

maintenance_signals.last_release: OKF v0.1 announced by Google Cloud on 2026-06-12
maintenance_signals.recent_activity: Early specification with published reference implementations and sample bundles; treat ecosystem maturity as evolving.
maintenance_signals.maintainer: Google Cloud Data Cloud team with open contributions
maintenance_signals.known_adoption: No production-fit claim recorded; evaluate on the target project's data and agent workflows.

items.install_or_use_shape: A directory of Markdown files with YAML front matter. It can live in the project repository and be read by humans or agents. No database migration, Google Cloud account, BigQuery access, SDK, or runtime dependency is required by the format itself.
risks: OKF is a knowledge representation, not a database, permission system, backup, data lineage engine, or access-control mechanism.
Schema descriptions, metrics, joins, and operational notes can be sensitive; publish only approved metadata and keep credentials, raw private data, and customer data out of the bundle.
The specification is early; validate local conventions, accuracy, freshness ownership, and agent retrieval quality before relying on it.
Do not infer live database access, query authority, or data freshness from an OKF document.
when_to_consider: When an agent must understand a project's database schema, business metrics, joins, data contracts, or operational runbooks repeatedly.
When database context is scattered across SQL, dashboards, docs, and team knowledge and needs a versioned, reviewable summary.
When a project wants a portable knowledge layer that does not depend on one cloud or agent vendor.
when_not_to_use: When the project has no durable data model or the cost of maintaining documentation outweighs its reuse value.
When a live schema introspection or existing authoritative catalog already serves the task and a duplicate bundle would drift.
When handling sensitive metadata has not been approved.
alternatives: existing data catalog or database documentation
a small reviewed Markdown data dictionary
source_evidence_refs: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/data-analytics/how-the-open-knowledge-format-can-improve-data-sharing/ - Google Cloud introduction to OKF v0.1, its Markdown/YAML bundle shape, database concept examples, and vendor-neutral design; inspected 2026-07-19.

https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf - reference code, specification, and example bundles linked from the Google Cloud announcement; inspect current repository state before selecting tooling.
items.status_reason: Indexed as an optional project-database knowledge-layer idea. It is not mandatory, selected for a project, installed, or integrated into any agent runtime.
items.next_review_action: For a concrete project, compare the bundle against the authoritative schema, define owners and freshness rules, classify sensitive metadata, and test whether it improves the agent's target tasks before relying on it.

items.name: withastro/flue
items.short_description: TypeScript framework for building autonomous agents and AI workflows with a programmable harness, tools, skills, sandboxes, sessions, deployment targets, and observability hooks.
items.why_it_matters: Useful as a reference architecture for agent harness design: how to combine instructions, typed tools, reusable skills, sandboxed execution, durable sessions, deployment surfaces, subagents, MCP, and observability.
agent_building_use_cases: Study harness/runtime boundaries for production agents.
Borrow ideas for typed tool packaging, skills, sandboxes, subagents, and deployment targets.
Compare with our runtime/devkit, birth, target-conformance, and long-running tool lifecycle layers.

maintenance_signals.recent_activity: active-looking repository; GitHub page showed 1,053 commits and public issues
maintenance_signals.known_adoption: GitHub page showed 7.1k stars and 410 forks during inspection

items.install_or_use_shape: Monorepo with @flue/runtime, @flue/cli, @flue/sdk, @flue/opentelemetry, and @flue/postgres packages; candidate for study before any dependency selection.
risks: Harness integration changes runtime architecture and should be treated as a separate project.
Sandbox, HTTP exposure, model keys, and connected tools require explicit permission and threat review.
Do not copy workflow prose or package structure into our runtime without target conformance and validation.
when_to_consider: When designing a TypeScript agent runtime or harness.
When comparing patterns for skills, tools, subagents, sandboxes, sessions, channels, MCP, and observability.
When building deployable agents that need explicit runtime composition.
when_not_to_use: When the task only needs a prompt/skill package for an existing IDE agent.
When target runtime is not TypeScript/Node and no harness migration is desired.
When there is no approval to add framework dependencies or hosted runtime surface.
source_evidence_refs: https://github.com/withastro/flue - README describes Flue as an agent harness framework with tools, skills, sandbox, sessions, deployment targets, MCP, and observability; inspected 2026-07-07.
items.status_reason: Primary GitHub repo inspected and basic metadata/use-case notes recorded. Not selected for any project and not integrated.
items.next_review_action: If considered for a concrete agent, review docs, package maturity, security model, deployment assumptions, and license compatibility.

items.name: PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR
items.short_description: OCR and document AI toolkit for converting PDFs and images into structured LLM-ready data such as JSON or Markdown, with multilingual recognition and document structure parsing.
items.why_it_matters: Useful for agents that need to read screenshots, scans, PDFs, tables, forms, multilingual images, document layouts, and RAG inputs from visual sources.
agent_building_use_cases: Document ingestion layer for RAG or research agents.
OCR step for screenshot/PDF processing workflows.
Structured extraction from tables, formulas, seals, charts, and scanned documents.
Potential MCP/document parsing reference because the repo contains an mcp_server folder.
language_or_runtime: optional JS bindings observed in repo tree

maintenance_signals.recent_activity: large active-looking repository; GitHub page showed 6,923 commits, issues, PRs, docs, tests, and multiple language docs
maintenance_signals.known_adoption: GitHub page showed 84.9k stars and 11k forks during inspection

items.install_or_use_shape: Python OCR/document parsing toolkit with models and APIs; integration likely requires model download/runtime setup and document privacy policy.
risks: OCR on private documents can expose sensitive data; needs explicit data handling rules.
Model/runtime dependencies may be heavy; GPU/CPU, install size, and performance need validation.
OCR outputs can be wrong; downstream agents need confidence, evidence refs, and human review for high-stakes documents.
Do not treat benchmark or README claims as proof for a target document set without evals.
when_to_consider: When an agent must ingest PDFs, scanned documents, screenshots, tables, or multilingual visual text.
When building document-to-Markdown/JSON pipelines for RAG or research workflows.
When closed-source OCR is not acceptable or local processing is preferred.
when_not_to_use: When the source is already clean text/HTML/Markdown.
When target environment cannot support Python/model dependencies.
When private document processing has no approved privacy/security policy.
source_evidence_refs: https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR - GitHub page describes OCR/document AI conversion to structured LLM-ready JSON/Markdown and 100+ language support; inspected 2026-07-07.
items.status_reason: Primary GitHub repo inspected and basic metadata/use-case notes recorded. Not selected for any project and not integrated.
items.next_review_action: Before integration, run target-document evals, check install/runtime size, privacy rules, model licenses, and output-quality gates.

items.name: Panniantong/Agent-Reach
items.short_description: CLI/capability layer that helps agents read and search web/social sources such as webpages, YouTube, RSS, search, GitHub, Twitter/X, Reddit, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu, LinkedIn, and others through selected upstream tools.
items.why_it_matters: Useful as a reference for agent-access bootstrapping, channel routing, doctor checks, and practical web/social research capabilities where native APIs are fragmented or paid.
agent_building_use_cases: Research agent acquisition layer for web/social/video/RSS/GitHub sources.
Reference design for capability routing: preferred backend plus fallback backend per platform.
Doctor/checkup pattern for diagnosing whether each acquisition channel works.
Skill-install pattern for teaching an agent when to call external upstream tools.

maintenance_signals.recent_activity: active-looking repository; GitHub page showed 306 commits, issues, PRs, tests, docs, changelog, and security file
maintenance_signals.known_adoption: GitHub page showed 52.2k stars and 4.2k forks during inspection

items.install_or_use_shape: Python CLI installed/configured by an agent; repo README describes install/update prompts, doctor command, and channel-specific upstream tools.
risks: Requires shell execution and package/system tool installation; never run during cataloging.
Some channels need cookies, browser login state, proxy, or platform-specific tooling; privacy and Terms-of-Service risk must be reviewed.
Web/social scraping routes can break or be blocked; needs fallback, doctor checks, and visible limitations.
Private account/session access requires explicit user approval and local-only credential handling.
when_to_consider: When a research agent needs practical web/social/video/RSS/GitHub acquisition beyond normal browser search.
When building an acquisition layer with channel health checks and fallbacks.
When a user explicitly approves command execution and source acquisition tooling.
when_not_to_use: When the workflow only needs a few stable public webpages that can be fetched directly.
When shell execution, login state, cookies, or scraping are not approved.
When legal/compliance constraints forbid platform scraping or session reuse.
source_evidence_refs: https://github.com/Panniantong/Agent-Reach - README describes a CLI capability layer for web/social/video/GitHub/RSS acquisition, doctor checks, supported platforms, and MIT/Python 3.10+ signals; inspected 2026-07-07.
items.status_reason: Primary GitHub repo inspected and basic metadata/use-case notes recorded. Not selected for any project and not integrated.
items.next_review_action: Before integration, review install script, permissions, channel-specific credentials, platform risks, and create acquisition-skip/approval records.
